meta.title: Analog CIM accuracy evidence ledger
meta.verdict: Scoped software-comparable accuracy has been demonstrated on measured chips. The general analog accuracy problem is not solved.
meta.reason: Successful demonstrations depend on a defined workload and a stack of mitigations such as write-verify, calibration, hardware-aware training, redundancy, weight expansion, mixed precision, digital execution, or periodic recalibration. Recent 2026 reviews still identify device, circuit, array, architecture, and lifetime non-idealities as barriers to large-scale deployment.

studies.title: Mythic current architecture and efficiency announcement
studies.scope: Next-generation roadmap and internal benchmark claims
studies.reportedResult: Claims 120 TOPS/W for the current APU architecture and roughly 100x system energy advantage versus top GPUs.
studies.whatItShows: Mythic's current product direction and the metrics the company considers central.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: A public test method, model-by-model accuracy table, independently reproduced end-to-end measurement, or production-yield distribution for the next-generation claim.
studies.verdict: Treat as a roadmap claim, not as independent proof.

studies.title: Equivalent-accuracy accelerated neural-network training using analogue memory
studies.scope: Mixed hardware-software training demonstrations up to 204,900 synapses
studies.reportedResult: Generalization accuracy equivalent to software training on MNIST, MNIST-backrand, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 in the reported mixed implementation.
studies.whatItShows: Update asymmetry and device variation can be countered with a carefully designed mixed memory unit and polarity inversion.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: A monolithic production training accelerator; the headline throughput and efficiency were calculated for the proposed implementation.
studies.verdict: A major algorithm-device result, not a universal end-to-end training solution.

studies.title: Fully hardware-implemented memristor convolutional neural network
studies.reportedResult: More than 96% MNIST accuracy with hybrid training adapted to device imperfections.
studies.whatItShows: A complete small CNN can run on fabricated memristor hardware with useful accuracy.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: Software parity on large modern datasets or models, long-term drift behavior, or general production scalability.
studies.verdict: Strong proof of concept at small task scale.

studies.title: A compute-in-memory chip based on resistive random-access memory
studies.scope: CNN, LSTM, and probabilistic models on complete datasets
studies.reportedResult: Fully hardware-measured 99.0% MNIST, 85.7% CIFAR-10, and 84.7% Google speech-command accuracy, comparable to software models quantized to 4-bit weights.
studies.whatItShows: Cross-layer co-design, calibration, measured-noise training, and chip-in-loop tuning can preserve low-bit software-level accuracy across several edge tasks.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: A fully self-contained product system; the reported board used an FPGA for intermediate buffers and partial sums, and larger modern workloads were not demonstrated.
studies.verdict: One of the clearest measured demonstrations, with a defined 4-bit baseline and system boundary.

studies.title: Gradient descent-based programming of analog in-memory computing cores
studies.technology: Two 14-nm PCM AIMC cores with 256 x 256 unit-cell arrays
studies.scope: Array-level programming, 24-hour drift observation, and a scaled ResNet-9 on CIFAR-10
studies.reportedResult: Gradient descent-based programming improved one measured ResNet-9 run from 84.75% to 86.01% accuracy, reduced MVM error relative to cell-wise iterative programming, and retained its programming-error advantage over 24 hours.
studies.whatItShows: Optimizing the measured MVM function with random probes can choose physical conductances that compensate small array nonlinearities, while avoiding accurate reads of individual low-current devices.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: A full SoC, broad model generality, population statistics, long-term retention, or a free correction: gradients were computed digitally, non-MVM operations ran in software, and repeated programming consumes time and endurance.
studies.verdict: A strong measured argument for function-level deployment calibration, not a solution to every read, converter, drift, or system error.

studies.title: A 64-core mixed-signal in-memory compute chip based on phase-change memory
studies.scope: ResNet and LSTM inference with on-chip activation and communication
studies.reportedResult: Near-software-equivalent accuracy, with up to 16.1 TOPS and 2.48 TOPS/W in high-precision mode or 63.1 TOPS and 9.76 TOPS/W in low-precision mode.
studies.whatItShows: A multicore AIMC chip can integrate analog MVM, digital activation, and on-chip communication rather than reporting an isolated macro.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: Universal parity across model families, large language models, all operating modes, or long deployment lifetimes.
studies.verdict: Strong integrated-chip evidence with an explicit precision-efficiency tradeoff.

studies.title: An analog-AI chip for energy-efficient speech recognition and transcription
studies.technology: 34-tile 14-nm PCM chip; five chips for RNNT
studies.scope: Keyword spotting and a 45-million-weight MLPerf RNNT
studies.reportedResult: KWS measured 86.14% versus an 86.75% software target. RNNT measured 9.258% word error rate versus a 7.452% FP software baseline after weight expansion; the paper classifies this as near software-equivalent.
studies.whatItShows: Commercially relevant speech workloads can tolerate analog error across many tiles and chips using hardware-aware training, differential mapping, and weight expansion.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: Exact FP parity. Some preprocessing and summation were digital or host-side, five chips were cascaded through saved outputs, and the full-system digital cost was estimated.
studies.verdict: A real scale-up milestone, but the mitigation and system boundary are part of the result.

studies.title: A mixed-precision memristor and SRAM compute-in-memory AI processor
studies.technology: Memristor CIM, SRAM CIM, and tiny digital units
studies.scope: ResNet-20 on CIFAR-100 and MobileNet-v2 on ImageNet
studies.reportedResult: Less than 0.45% accuracy degradation with measured energy efficiency of 40.91 TFLOPS/W and 28.63 TFLOPS/W for the two reported workloads.
studies.whatItShows: Layer- and kernel-level sensitivity mapping can combine analog density with precise SRAM and digital execution to preserve accuracy.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: That a homogeneous pure-analog path is accurate. Moving sensitive work to other compute domains is the central solution.
studies.verdict: Accuracy is effectively handled by heterogeneity, which is a practical system answer rather than a device-level cure.

studies.title: Demonstration of transformer-based ALBERT model on a 14nm analog AI inference chip
studies.scope: ALBERT with 7.1 million unique weights shared across 12 layers and seven GLUE tasks
studies.reportedResult: Average hardware accuracy was 1.8% below the floating-point reference; hardware-aware fine-tuning improved average accuracy by 4.4%. Recalibration reduced accuracy loss from roughly 5% conductance drift over 30 days to under 1%.
studies.whatItShows: A meaningfully sized transformer's fully connected weights can be mapped to analog hardware with near-iso accuracy and controlled drift.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: End-to-end transformer silicon. Attention calculations, activation functions, pooling, and classification were performed in software, and only subsets of some large GLUE datasets were tested on hardware.
studies.verdict: Important transformer feasibility evidence, explicitly not an end-to-end LLM result.

studies.title: Memristor-based adaptive analog-to-digital conversion for efficient and accurate compute-in-memory
studies.scope: Converter prototype and model evaluation under measured device variation
studies.reportedResult: A 5-bit ADC reached 89.55% on VGG8/CIFAR-10 under experimental variations; a super-resolution strategy reached 65.50% for 6-bit ResNet-18/ImageNet evaluation.
studies.whatItShows: Adaptive thresholds and paired-converter variation can reduce the ADC energy-area-accuracy penalty.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: A complete large-model AIMC accelerator with all arrays, routing, storage, and digital operations measured end to end.
studies.verdict: A useful converter-level mitigation, not a blanket solution to analog error.

studies.title: Fault-free analogue computing with imperfect hardware
studies.scope: DFT, image processing, and wireless baseband matrix operations
studies.reportedResult: More than 99.999% cosine similarity for a DFT matrix despite a 39% device fault rate, plus a 56-fold bit-error-rate reduction in a wireless prototype.
studies.whatItShows: Factoring a target matrix into two adjustable submatrices can route around many stuck devices and avoid conventional differential-pair mapping.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: That random read noise, ADC error, nonlinear arrays, long-term drift, training updates, or large DNN end-to-end accuracy are all solved. Additional compensation layers improve accuracy but consume resources.
studies.verdict: A powerful static-fault representation technique, not a universal analog-accuracy theorem.

studies.title: Strategies of high-accuracy memristor-based analogue computing in memory for artificial intelligence
studies.scope: Device, array, circuit, architecture, and algorithm mitigation survey
studies.reportedResult: The review states that analog CIM still struggles with high accuracy and that many mitigations add hardware or energy overhead.
studies.whatItShows: The field now has a rich mitigation toolbox and a clearer cross-layer roadmap.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: A single technique that removes all accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs at deployment scale.
studies.verdict: Direct evidence against the broad claim that accuracy is already solved.

studies.title: Non-idealities in artificial synapses
studies.scope: Nonlinearity, asymmetry, variability, endurance, and retention
studies.reportedResult: The review catalogs persistent non-ideal effects that limit reliable accelerator integration and argues for system-aware use of imperfections.
studies.whatItShows: Device behavior must be characterized in the context of the target system and workload.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: A universal error-free device or a cost-free correction method.
studies.verdict: The most current review evidence says the core reliability questions remain active.

studies.title: Heterogeneous Mapping for Analog In-Memory Computing Accelerators: A Unified Workflow
studies.venue: IEEE Computer Architecture Letters manuscript / arXiv preprint
studies.technology: Modeled PCM-calibrated AIMC tiles plus digital compute units
studies.scope: Survey of nine mapping methods and Stage 1-2 sensitivity analysis of GPT-2-small
studies.reportedResult: Four of 49 GPT-2 projections dominated modeled sensitivity; 40 projections had a perplexity increase below 3 when perturbed individually, suggesting about 82% projection-level analog candidacy under the paper's threshold.
studies.whatItShows: Projection-level sensitivity profiling can prune an otherwise combinatorial analog/digital mapping search, and a first/last-layer rule misses a highly sensitive internal projection in decoder transformers.
studies.whatItDoesNotShow: A completed Stage 3 mapping, joint multi-layer error, measured hardware, end-to-end energy or latency, drift-aware runtime adaptation, or validation on billion-parameter LLMs and other memory technologies.
studies.verdict: A useful and current compiler workflow hypothesis; its GPT-2 mapping conclusions still require system-level optimization and silicon validation.

issues.symptom: The programmed conductance misses the target weight.
issues.rootCause: Finite update granularity, stochastic switching, and cell-to-cell response differences.
issues.mitigations: Iterative write-verify, closed-loop programming, better materials, per-device models.
issues.cost: Longer programming time, extra read cycles, controller complexity, and possible endurance use.

issues.symptom: Stuck-on or stuck-off cells create large structured errors.
issues.mitigations: Defect maps, spare rows/columns, remapping, matrix factorization, analog ECC, retraining.
issues.cost: Redundant devices, extra arrays or MVM stages, and optimization time.

issues.symptom: Accuracy decays over hours, days, temperature changes, or power cycles.
issues.rootCause: Physical state relaxation and charge or resistance drift.
issues.mitigations: Drift-aware training, reference cells, gain recalibration, selective refresh, periodic reprogramming.
issues.cost: Telemetry, calibration data, maintenance energy, downtime, and lifetime management.

issues.symptom: Repeated MVMs return a distribution rather than one exact value.
issues.rootCause: Thermal, shot, flicker, supply, coupling, sense, and timing noise.
issues.mitigations: Hardware-aware noise training, larger signal range, averaging, weight expansion, differential readout.
issues.cost: More rows or devices, longer integration, lower throughput, and increased energy.

issues.symptom: Cells far from drivers behave differently and sums become input dependent.
issues.rootCause: Word-line and bit-line resistance, source resistance, sneak paths, and capacitive coupling.
issues.mitigations: Smaller arrays, access transistors, low read voltage, IR-drop-aware mapping and calibration.
issues.cost: Lower density, more tiling, more converters, and more partial-sum traffic.

issues.symptom: Ohm's-law multiplication or positive/negative weight updates are biased.
issues.rootCause: Nonlinear I-V curves and state-dependent programming response.
issues.mitigations: Operate in a linear region, differential or offset encoding, pulse shaping, device-aware training.
issues.cost: Reduced signal range, doubled cells, or more complex write circuitry.

issues.symptom: Quantization, offset, gain error, or saturation limits effective precision.
issues.rootCause: Finite converter resolution and wide layer-dependent dynamic ranges.
issues.mitigations: Per-column calibration, adaptive thresholds, bit slicing, multiple phases, layer-specific ranges.
issues.cost: Converter area and energy, extra cycles, metadata, and calibration complexity.

issues.symptom: A few large weights or activations consume dynamic range and bury small values in noise.
issues.rootCause: Heavy-tailed tensor distributions, especially in transformer projections.
issues.mitigations: Clipping, channel scaling, outlier splitting, mixed precision, weight expansion, digital routing.
issues.cost: Retraining, duplicated work, extra digital compute, or lower analog utilization.

issues.symptom: Peak array TOPS/W collapses at the application level.
issues.rootCause: Irregular shapes, partial sums, underfilled arrays, routing, and unsupported operators.
issues.mitigations: Graph compilation, packing, fusion, balanced pipelines, local SRAM, heterogeneous execution.
issues.cost: Compiler complexity and a larger digital subsystem.

issues.symptom: Frequent weight updates wear devices or become too inaccurate.
issues.rootCause: Limited write endurance, nonlinear/asymmetric updates, and required high-precision accumulation.
issues.mitigations: Inference-only deployment, mixed-precision training, digital accumulators, periodic transfer, robust optimizers.
issues.cost: Less in-memory work and reduced theoretical training speed or energy advantage.

issues.symptom: Accuracy and latency vary across process, voltage, temperature, age, and chips.
issues.mitigations: Corner-aware design, on-chip sensors, characterization, guardbands, per-chip calibration, burn-in and binning.
issues.cost: Test time, yield loss, field-calibration infrastructure, and conservative operating points.

claimTests.question: Was the result measured on fabricated silicon, derived from device traces, or simulated?
claimTests.answer: All three evidence types appear in this literature and must not be merged. NeuRRAM reports fabricated-chip measurements for every benchmark result, and the HERMES and speech studies also measure fabricated 14-nm PCM chips. The 2018 training study is a mixed hardware-software experiment whose throughput and energy figures are calculated for the proposed implementation. The adaptive-ADC study characterizes devices but uses circuit and neural-network simulation for important system results, while the 2026 GPT-2 mapping study is a PCM-calibrated noise-model analysis with no target chip measurement.
claimTests.limit: A result can use real silicon and still leave operators, data movement, or system energy outside the measured boundary.

claimTests.question: Did the complete validation dataset run through hardware, or only selected layers and samples?
claimTests.answer: The answer is study-specific. NeuRRAM explicitly says all benchmark results came from hardware measurements on complete datasets. The five-chip RNNT experiment sent the full LibriSpeech validation set through chip 1 and then through the remaining chips, but intermediate outputs were saved on an x86 host while chips were swapped. In the ALBERT study, the complete validation sets were used for RTE, MRPC, CoLA, and SST-2; QNLI, MNLI, and QQP used randomized 1,000-sequence subsets selected so that their floating-point accuracy matched the full datasets.
claimTests.limit: A representative subset is useful evidence, but it is not the same claim as complete-dataset hardware validation and should be reported separately.

sources.label: ALBERT full sets and selected subsets

claimTests.question: Is the baseline FP32, a quantized software model, a software-equivalent threshold, or the authors' own retrained model?
claimTests.answer: There is no common baseline. NeuRRAM's main comparison is a software model with 4-bit weights, not FP32. The RNNT paper reports a 7.452% word-error-rate software baseline, defines a looser 99%-software-equivalent threshold of 8.378%, and obtains 9.258% after weight expansion. ALBERT starts from a floating-point model, performs hardware-aware fine-tuning in software, and reports hardware accuracy relative to the floating-point reference. The mixed memristor-SRAM processor reports degradation relative to an FP16 software baseline. Therefore, the phrase 'near software accuracy' is not comparable across papers without the adjacent baseline definition.
claimTests.limit: Always record the baseline precision, training recipe, acceptance threshold, metric, and absolute result beside the hardware number.

claimTests.question: Which work remained off-chip or in software: attention, activation, normalization, pooling, routing, or partial sums?
claimTests.answer: The boundaries are documented and differ sharply. NeuRRAM maps convolution, fully connected, and batch-normalization parameters to one chip, while an on-board FPGA performs partial-sum accumulation, average pooling, and LSTM element-wise operations. In the RNNT experiment, analog MACs run on the PCM chips, while vector-vector products, nonlinear activations, biases, the joint layer, and affine calibration run on an x86 host. ALBERT maps four fully connected layer blocks to analog hardware; attention matrices, GELU, softmax, tanh, pooling, and classification remain in software. HERMES is more integrated and implements weight-layer computation, activation functions, and on-chip communication.
claimTests.limit: Count preprocessing, unsupported operators, intermediate storage, host transfers, control, and partial sums before calling a demonstration end to end.

claimTests.question: How much retraining, chip-in-loop tuning, calibration, redundancy, expansion, or refresh was required?
claimTests.answer: The mitigation is substantial and is part of the result. NeuRRAM combines per-layer model-driven calibration using training samples, measured-noise training, analog write programming, and progressive chip-in-loop fine-tuning. The RNNT work states that it did not retrain the network, but it uses affine tile calibration, differential mapping, two or four PCM devices per weight, and optional weight expansion. ALBERT uses closed-loop programming, four PCM devices per weight, hardware-aware fine-tuning that improves average hardware accuracy by 4.4%, per-use column scale and offset calibration, and later recalibration for drift. Gradient-descent programming adds repeated random-probe MVMs and digitally calculated gradients during deployment programming.
claimTests.limit: Training time, calibration data, programming cycles, extra devices, metadata, refresh cadence, and endurance consumption belong in the implementation cost.

claimTests.question: Are the reported energy and latency measured end to end, or only for a full and perfectly utilized array?
claimTests.answer: Usually the boundary is narrower than wall-plug application energy. The 2018 training paper explicitly calculates its projected energy efficiency and throughput density. HERMES reports maximum full-chip MVM throughput and efficiency for high- and low-precision modes. The speech study measures each chip's power, but estimates the missing digital processing and simulates integrated-system execution time; communication, low tile utilization, and digital work reduce the analog-tile figure from 20.0 TOPS/W to a projected sustained 6.94 TOPS/W. ALBERT experimentally measures analog MAC time and power while simulating non-MAC digital units in 14-nm technology. The mixed memristor-SRAM processor reports measured processor efficiency, but that remains different from board or application wall-plug energy.
claimTests.limit: Report array, full-chip, board, and end-to-end application numbers separately, together with utilization, batch size, data movement, and modeled components.

sources.label: Speech chip measurement and system estimate
ALBERT measured analog and simulated digital power

claimTests.question: How does accuracy change across chips, temperature, voltage, process corners, defects, and time?
claimTests.answer: Only fragments of this production question are answered. ALBERT measured one MRPC workload for 30 days at room temperature: accuracy fell by roughly 5% without recalibration and by less than 1% with recalibration. The paper says longer retention would require elevated-temperature testing. The 2020 memristor CNN includes a two-hour drift study and one million read pulses. The 2026 fault-free method tolerates a measured 39% static device-fault rate for a DFT matrix, but that does not cover dynamic read noise, voltage, process corners, or aging. The 2026 reviews still list variability, endurance, retention, and cross-layer non-idealities as deployment barriers.
claimTests.limit: The reviewed showcase papers do not provide a common multi-chip, wafer-level PVT, yield, and lifetime distribution for their headline neural-network result.

sources.label: Memristor CNN drift and read-disturb tests

claimTests.question: Does the mitigation preserve density and efficiency, or move the cost into more arrays, converters, digital work, or extra cycles?
claimTests.answer: Most methods move at least part of the cost. ALBERT uses four PCM devices per logical weight plus calibration. RNNT mappings use two or four PCM devices per weight; weight expansion improves accuracy but changes the analog-to-digital operation ratio from 325:1 to 88:1. The mixed-CIM processor preserves accuracy by routing sensitive kernels to SRAM CIM or tiny digital units rather than curing the memristor path. Fault-free representation replaces one target matrix with two optimized factors, trading extra stages for fault tolerance, although it reports gains against differential-pair baselines. The adaptive ADC reduces modeled converter overhead, but an n-bit flash-like design requires 2^(n-1) quantization cells. The 2026 accuracy review concludes that many mitigations add hardware or energy overhead.
claimTests.limit: Compare usable weight density, converter area and energy, extra MVM phases, digital fallback, calibration traffic, and achieved utilization after mitigation.

claimTests.question: Is the claim for inference, training, or both? Static inference weights avoid the update linearity and endurance burden of training.
claimTests.answer: The major recent chip demonstrations in this ledger are inference results. The 2018 Nature work is the principal training example, but it is a mixed hardware-software system using volatile capacitors for near-linear updates and PCM for long-term storage; its throughput and energy are projected for the proposed implementation. The 2020 CNN uses hybrid training to adapt programmed hardware. ALBERT explicitly targets inference and notes that training introduces programming-symmetry, update-linearity, and endurance requirements not evaluated there. Gradient-descent programming optimizes deployed array conductances before inference; it is not end-to-end neural-network training on the AIMC core.
claimTests.limit: Do not use an inference accuracy result as evidence that the same memory can sustain accurate, frequent in-situ training updates.

claimTests.question: Can the method scale to the target model's parameter count, tensor shapes, context length, and communication pattern?
claimTests.answer: There is progress but no general large-LLM proof. The RNNT experiment places about 45 million weights across five chips and processes the full validation set, with host-assisted sequencing. ALBERT maps 7.1 million unique weights, shared across 12 layers, into 28.3 million programmed PCM devices on one chip; its GLUE sequences are at most 128 tokens, while longer sequences up to 1,000 appear only in power analysis. The 2026 mapping paper studies GPT-2-small with 124 million parameters, but converts one projection at a time under a modeled PCM error and completes only sensitivity stages, not joint mapping or silicon validation. It identifies KV-cache capacity, prefill/decode asymmetry, tiling, communication, joint layer error, and billion-parameter search as open problems.
claimTests.limit: No paper in this ledger demonstrates a billion-parameter autoregressive LLM, long-context KV-cache traffic, and end-to-end accuracy, latency, and energy on fabricated analog CIM hardware.

sources.label: Single-chip ALBERT scale and sequence length
GPT-2 mapping scope and open challenges
